content:
# Genesis Chapter 49 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 28v–29r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_049_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_049_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), 4th century CE | **LXX Images:** `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/GEN_049_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/GEN_049_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`
**View Vaticanus online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0053 | https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0054

---

*Jacob Summons His Sons — The Final Blessing (vv. 1–2)*

**49:1** And Jacob called [וַיִּקְרָא יַעֲקֹב] to his sons [אֶל-בָּנָיו] and said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Gather yourselves together [הֵאָסְפוּ] and I will tell you [וְאַגִּידָה לָכֶם] what will befall you [אֵת אֲשֶׁר-יִקְרָא אֶתְכֶם] in the latter days [בְּאַחֲרִית הַיָּמִים].

**49:2** Assemble yourselves and hear [הִקָּבְצוּ וְשִׁמְעוּ] — O sons of Jacob [בְּנֵי יַעֲקֹב] — and hearken to Israel your father [וְשִׁמְעוּ אֶל-יִשְׂרָאֵל אֲבִיכֶם].

---

*The Oracle of Reuben — First-Born but Forfeited (vv. 3–4)*

**49:3** Reuben [רְאוּבֵן] — you are my firstborn [בְּכֹרִי אַתָּה] — my might [כֹּחִי] and the beginning of my strength [וְרֵאשִׁית אוֹנִי] — preeminent in dignity [יֶתֶר שְׂאֵת] and preeminent in power [וְיֶתֶר עָז].

**49:4** Reckless as water [פַּחַז כַּמַּיִם] — you shall not have preeminence [אַל-תּוֹתַר] — for you went up [כִּי עָלִיתָ] to your father's bed [מִשְׁכְּבֵי אָבִיךָ] — then you defiled it [אָז חִלַּלְתָּ] — he went up [עָלָה] to my couch [יְצוּעִי].

---

*The Oracle of Simeon and Levi — Instruments of Violence (vv. 5–7)*

**49:5** Simeon and Levi [שִׁמְעוֹן וְלֵוִי] are brothers [אַחִים] — instruments of violence [כְּלֵי חָמָס] are their swords [מְכֵרֹתֵיהֶם].

**49:6** Into their council let not my soul enter [בְּסֹדָם אַל-תָּבֹא נַפְשִׁי] — in their assembly let not my glory be joined [בִּקְהָלָם אַל-תֵּחַד כְּבֹדִי] — for in their anger they killed men [כִּי בְאַפָּם הָרְגוּ אִישׁ] — and in their willfulness they hamstrung oxen [וּבִרְצֹנָם עִקְּרוּ-שׁוֹר].

**49:7** Cursed be their anger [אָרוּר אַפָּם] for it is fierce [כִּי עָז] — and their fury [וְעֶבְרָתָם] for it is cruel [כִּי קָשָׁתָה] — I will divide them in Jacob [אֲחַלְּקֵם בְּיַעֲקֹב] and scatter them in Israel [וַאֲפִיצֵם בְּיִשְׂרָאֵל].

---

*The Oracle of Judah — The Scepter and the Lion (vv. 8–12)*

**49:8** Judah [יְהוּדָה] — your brothers shall praise you [יוֹדוּךָ אַחֶיךָ] — your hand shall be on the neck of your enemies [יָדְךָ בְּעֹרֶף אֹיְבֶיךָ] — the sons of your father shall bow down to you [יִשְׁתַּחֲווּ לְךָ בְּנֵי אָבִיךָ].

**49:9** A lion's cub is Judah [גּוּר אַרְיֵה יְהוּדָה] — from the prey my son you have gone up [מִטֶּרֶף בְּנִי עָלִיתָ] — he crouched down [כָּרַע] — he lay down like a lion [רָבַץ כְּאַרְיֵה] — and like a lioness [וּכְלָבִיא] — who shall rouse him [מִי יְקִימֶנּוּ]?

**49:10** The scepter shall not depart from Judah [לֹא-יָסוּר שֵׁבֶט מִיהוּדָה] nor the ruler's staff from between his feet [וּמְחֹקֵק מִבֵּין רַגְלָיו] — until Shiloh comes [עַד כִּי-יָבֹא שִׁילֹה] — and to him shall be the obedience of the peoples [וְלוֹ יִקְּהַת עַמִּים].

**49:11** Binding his foal to the vine [אֹסְרִי לַגֶּפֶן עִירֹה] — and his donkey's colt to the choice vine [וְלַשֹּׂרֵקָה בְּנִי אֲתֹנוֹ] — he washes his garments in wine [כִּבֵּס בַּיַּיִן לְבֻשׁוֹ] — and his robe in the blood of grapes [וּבְדַם-עֲנָבִים סוּתֹה].

**49:12** His eyes are darker than wine [חַכְלִילִי עֵינַיִם מִיָּיִן] — and his teeth whiter than milk [וּלְבֶן-שִׁנַּיִם מֵחָלָב].

---

*The Oracle of Zebulun — Seashore and Ships (v. 13)*

**49:13** Zebulun [זְבוּלֻן] shall dwell at the shore of the sea [לְחוֹף יַמִּים יִשְׁכֹּן] — and he shall be a shore for ships [וְהוּא לְחוֹף אֳנִיֹּת] — and his flank toward Sidon [וְיַרְכָתוֹ עַל-צִידֹן].

---

*The Oracle of Issachar — The Bony Donkey (vv. 14–15)*

**49:14** Issachar [יִשָּׂשכָר] is a strong [גָּרֶם] donkey [חֲמֹר] — crouching between the sheepfolds [רֹבֵץ בֵּין הַמִּשְׁפְּתָיִם].

**49:15** And he saw that rest was good [וַיַּרְא מְנֻחָה כִּי טוֹב] — and that the land was pleasant [וְאֶת-הָאָרֶץ כִּי נָעֵמָה] — and he bowed his shoulder to bear [וַיֵּט שִׁכְמוֹ לִסְבֹּל] — and he became a forced-laborer [וַיְהִי לְמַס-עֹבֵד].

---

*The Oracle of Dan — Serpent on the Path (vv. 16–18)*

**49:16** Dan shall judge his people [דָּן יָדִין עַמּוֹ] as one of the tribes of Israel [כְּאַחַד שִׁבְטֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל].

**49:17** Let Dan be a serpent by the way [יְהִי-דָן נָחָשׁ עֲלֵי-דֶרֶךְ] — a horned viper on the path [שְׁפִיפֹן עֲלֵי-אֹרַח] — that bites the horse's heels [הַנֹּשֵׁךְ עִקְּבֵי-סוּס] so that its rider falls backward [וַיִּפֹּל רֹכְבוֹ אָחוֹר].

**49:18** For your salvation I wait, O LORD [לִישׁוּעָתְךָ קִוִּיתִי יְהוָה].

---

*The Oracle of Gad — Raiders and the Raided (v. 19)*

**49:19** Gad [גָּד] — a raiding band shall raid him [גְּדוּד יְגוּדֶנּוּ] — but he shall raid at their heel [וְהוּא יָגֻד עָקֵב].

---

*The Oracle of Asher — Rich Bread and Royal Dainties (v. 20)*

**49:20** From Asher [מֵאָשֵׁר] — his bread shall be rich [שְׁמֵנָה לַחְמוֹ] — and he shall yield royal dainties [וְהוּא יִתֵּן מַעֲדַנֵּי-מֶלֶךְ].

---

*The Oracle of Naphtali — The Doe Set Free (v. 21)*

**49:21** Naphtali [נַפְתָּלִי] is a doe let loose [אַיָּלָה שְׁלֻחָה] — who gives beautiful words [הַנֹּתֵן אִמְרֵי-שָׁפֶר].

---

*The Oracle of Joseph — Son of Fruitfulness and the Mighty One (vv. 22–26)*

**49:22** A son of fruitfulness is Joseph [בֵּן פֹּרָת יוֹסֵף] — a son of fruitfulness by a spring [בֵּן פֹּרָת עֲלֵי-עָיִן] — daughters stride over a wall [בָּנוֹת צָעֲדָה עֲלֵי-שׁוּר].

**49:23** And the archers bitterly attacked him [וַיְמָרְרֻהוּ וָרֹבּוּ] — and they shot at him [וַיִּשְׂטְמֻהוּ] — the masters of arrows [בַּעֲלֵי חִצִּים].

**49:24** Yet his bow remained firm [וַתֵּשֶׁב בְּאֵיתָן קַשְׁתּוֹ] — and the arms of his hands were made supple [וַיָּפֹזּוּ זְרֹעֵי יָדָיו] — from the hands of the Mighty One of Jacob [מִידֵי אֲבִיר יַעֲקֹב] — from there is the Shepherd, the Stone of Israel [מִשָּׁם רֹעֶה אֶבֶן יִשְׂרָאֵל] —

**49:25** from the God of your father who will help you [מֵאֵל אָבִיךָ וְיַעְזְרֶךָּ] — and from El Shaddai who will bless you [וְאֵת שַׁדַּי וִיבָרְכֶךָּ] — with blessings of the heavens above [בִּרְכֹת שָׁמַיִם מֵעָל] — with blessings of the deep that crouches below [בִּרְכֹת תְּהוֹם רֹבֶצֶת תָּחַת] — with blessings of the breasts and of the womb [בִּרְכֹת שָׁדַיִם וָרָחַם].

**49:26** The blessings of your father prevail [בִּרְכֹת אָבִיךָ גָּבְרוּ] over the blessings of my progenitors [עַל-בִּרְכֹת הוֹרַי] — to the bound of the everlasting hills [עַד-תַּאֲוַת גִּבְעֹת עוֹלָם] — they shall be on the head of Joseph [תִּהְיֶיןָ לְרֹאשׁ יוֹסֵף] — and on the crown of the consecrated one of his brothers [וּלְקָדְקֹד נְזִיר אֶחָיו].

---

*The Oracle of Benjamin — A Wolf That Tears (v. 27)*

**49:27** Benjamin [בִּנְיָמִין] is a wolf that tears [זְאֵב יִטְרָף] — in the morning he devours prey [בַּבֹּקֶר יֹאכַל עַד] — and in the evening he divides plunder [וְלָעֶרֶב יְחַלֵּק שָׁלָל].

---

*Summary — All Twelve Tribes; Jacob's Burial Command (vv. 28–32)*

**49:28** All these are the twelve tribes of Israel [כָּל-אֵלֶּה שִׁבְטֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל שְׁנֵים עָשָׂר] — and this is what their father spoke to them [וְזֹאת אֲשֶׁר-דִּבֶּר לָהֶם אֲבִיהֶם] — and he blessed them [וַיְבָרֶךְ אֹתָם] — each according to his blessing he blessed them [אִישׁ אֲשֶׁר כְּבִרְכָתוֹ בֵּרַךְ אֹתָם].

**49:29** And he commanded them [וַיְצַו אוֹתָם] and said to them [וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם]: I am being gathered to my people [אֲנִי נֶאֱסָף אֶל-עַמִּי] — bury me with my fathers [קִבְרוּ אֹתִי אֶל-אֲבֹתַי] — in the cave [אֶל-הַמְּעָרָה] that is in the field of Ephron the Hittite [אֲשֶׁר בִּשְׂדֵה עֶפְרֹן הַחִתִּי].

**49:30** In the cave that is in the field of Machpelah [בַּמְּעָרָה אֲשֶׁר בִּשְׂדֵה הַמַּכְפֵּלָה] — which is before Mamre [אֲשֶׁר עַל-פְּנֵי מַמְרֵא] — in the land of Canaan [בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן] — which Abraham bought [אֲשֶׁר קָנָה אַבְרָהָם] together with the field [אֶת-הַשָּׂדֶה] from Ephron the Hittite [מֵאֵת עֶפְרֹן הַחִתִּי] — as a possession for a burial place [לַאֲחֻזַּת-קָבֶר].

**49:31** There they buried Abraham and Sarah his wife [שָׁמָּה קָבְרוּ אֶת-אַבְרָהָם וְאֵת שָׂרָה אִשְׁתּוֹ] — there they buried Isaac and Rebekah his wife [שָׁמָּה קָבְרוּ אֶת-יִצְחָק וְאֵת רִבְקָה אִשְׁתּוֹ] — and there I buried Leah [וְשָׁמָּה קָבַרְתִּי אֶת-לֵאָה].

**49:32** The purchase of the field and the cave within it [מִקְנֵה הַשָּׂדֶה וְהַמְּעָרָה אֲשֶׁר-בּוֹ] — from the sons of Heth [מֵאֵת בְּנֵי-חֵת].

---

*Jacob Breathes His Last — Gathered to His People (v. 33)*

**49:33** And Jacob finished commanding his sons [וַיְכַל יַעֲקֹב לְצַוֹּת אֶת-בָּנָיו] — and he gathered his feet into the bed [וַיֶּאֱסֹף רַגְלָיו אֶל-הַמִּטָּה] — and he breathed his last [וַיִּגְוַע] — and was gathered to his people [וַיֵּאָסֶף אֶל-עַמָּיו].

---

## Philological Notes

**1. be-acharit ha-yamim (49:1) — "in the latter days" [בְּאַחֲרִית הַיָּמִים]**
The formula be-acharit ha-yamim is an eschatological or prophetic terminus technicus meaning literally "in the end of the days" or "in the after-part of the days." It does not necessarily denote the absolute eschatological end-time (as in later apocalyptic usage) but marks a future horizon beyond the speaker's present — the era in which the tribal destinies will be realized. The same formula opens Balaam's fourth oracle (Num 24:14), Moses's final address (Deut 31:29), and several prophetic passages (Isa 2:2; Mic 4:1; Jer 23:20). Jacob's blessing is thus framed as prophetic oracle, not paternal advice. The LXX renders ἐπ' ἐσχάτων τῶν ἡμερῶν, "in the last of the days," introducing a temporal absoluteness not present in the Hebrew construct phrase.

**2. pachaz ka-mayim (49:4) — "reckless as water" [פַּחַז כַּמַּיִם]**
The word pachaz is a hapax legomenon in the Hebrew Bible, appearing only here. Its precise meaning must be inferred from cognate languages and context. The Arabic root fhz denotes "haste, recklessness"; Aramaic uses cognates for "lightness, frivolity." The standard English renderings oscillate between "unstable," "turbulent," "reckless," and "boiling." The translation "reckless as water" captures both the moral connotation (the Arabic/Aramaic cognates pointing to irresponsibility, wanton behavior) and the physical image (water that cannot be contained or directed). The grammar is unusual — pachaz is likely a noun used in apposition ("recklessness of water"), functioning as a predicate nominative. The charge references Reuben's violation of Bilhah (Gen 35:22), for which his preeminence as firstborn is forfeited without explicit narrative penalty at the time; the blessing-oracle delivers the delayed verdict.

**3. mekherteihem (49:5) — "their swords" [מְכֵרֹתֵיהֶם]**
The word mekherteihem is a crux interpretum of the first order. It occurs only here. Ancient versions diverge dramatically: the LXX reads "they completed their iniquity by choice" (συνετέλεσαν ἀδικίαν ἐξ αἱρέσεως αὐτῶν), which presupposes a different Vorlage. The Vulgate reads "vessels of iniquity" (vasa iniquitatis). The Syriac Peshitta reads "their swords" (seyf). Most modern scholars derive the word from a root related to makhir ("merchant, trader") or mkr — giving either "swords" (from an assumed cognate with Akkadian nakkaru, "to cut") or "plans/devices." The BHS apparatus suggests emending to mekheroteihem ("their trade-goods/instruments"). The translation renders "swords" following the Syriac and the contextual parallelism with kelei chamas ("instruments of violence") — the reference to the Shechem massacre (Gen 34) involves literal killing.

**4. ad ki-yavo Shiloh (49:10) — "until Shiloh comes" [עַד כִּי-יָבֹא שִׁילֹה]**
The Shiloh clause is the most debated phrase in Genesis, with three major reading traditions:
(a) Shiloh as a proper noun — the cult city of Shiloh (Josh 18:1; 1 Sam 1–4), a geographical reference to Judean-territory political consolidation.
(b) Shiloh as a messianic title — "the one to whom it belongs" — by dividing shiloh into the relative particle she- + loh ("whose it is"), yielding "until he comes whose it is," which matches Ezek 21:32 (MT: "until he comes whose right it is"). This reading is supported by some ancient versions and the majority of classical Jewish and Christian messianic interpretation.
(c) Shiloh as a contraction of shai + loh ("tribute to him"), yielding "until tribute comes to him," a reading supported by some Targum traditions (Onkelos renders "until the Messiah comes").
The LXX Vaticanus reads ἕως ἂν ἔλθῃ τὰ ἀποκείμενα αὐτῷ ("until the things reserved for him come"), which reflects the "whose it is" or "what is stored up for him" interpretation. The MT consonantal text shylh is rendered literally without interpretation, in keeping with EMET's mandate to preserve the Hebrew. The note documents the textual plurality; the rendering "until Shiloh comes" makes no hermeneutical commitment.

**5. yiqhat ammim (49:10) — "the obedience of the peoples" [יִקְּהַת עַמִּים]**
The noun yiqhat is another near-hapax, appearing only here and in Prov 30:17 (with the sense of "obeying/attending to one's parents"). It derives from a root meaning "to obey, heed, attend to." The construct phrase yiqhat ammim means "the obedience of peoples" — i.e., peoples who obey him, or the act of peoples' obedience directed toward Judah's ruler. The LXX renders προσδοκία ἐθνῶν ("the expectation of the nations"), which captures the future-oriented, eschatological nuance and was taken up in later messianic reading. The MT yiqhat carries the more concrete sense of obedient submission; "obedience" is retained.

**6. garem chamor (49:14) — "a strong donkey" [חֲמֹר גָּרֶם]**
The word garem (MT order: chamor garem, "a donkey of bone/strong") is a hapax in this adjectival usage. The root grm means "bone" (cf. atsem) and denominatively, "strong, sturdy, bony." The image is of a large, heavy-boned pack donkey — sturdy and load-bearing but with a certain stubbornness. LXX renders ὑποζύγιον καλόν ("a fine/good beast of burden"), missing the specific connotation of stocky strength. The translation "a strong donkey" follows the structural parallel with "crouching between the sheepfolds" — both images depict settled, burden-bearing contentment at the cost of freedom.

**7. avir Ya'akov (49:24) — "the Mighty One of Jacob" [אֲבִיר יַעֲקֹב]**
The divine title avir Ya'akov, "the Mighty One of Jacob," is one of the oldest Israelite divine epithets, appearing here for the first time and recurring throughout the Psalter (Ps 132:2, 5) and Isaiah (Isa 1:24; 49:26; 60:16). The root avir means "strong, mighty" and is cognate with the word for "bull" (abbir), suggesting a background in ancient Near Eastern bovine iconography of divine strength. The Joseph oracle deploys three stacked divine titles in sequence: avir Ya'akov — ro'eh even Yisra'el — El Shaddai — El Avikha, a density unmatched elsewhere in Genesis. The title is rendered "the Mighty One of Jacob" throughout; "the Bull of Jacob" (historically defensible but overly interpretive) is not used.

**8. ro'eh even Yisra'el (49:24) — "the Shepherd, the Stone of Israel" [רֹעֶה אֶבֶן יִשְׂרָאֵל]**
The phrase ro'eh even Yisra'el is grammatically ambiguous in two ways. First, the relationship between ro'eh ("shepherd") and even ("stone"): if read as a construct chain, it would yield "the Shepherd of the Stone of Israel" (awkward). If read as two appositive titles — "the Shepherd, the Stone of Israel" — both nouns serve as divine epithets independently. The construct relationship ro'eh/even is difficult; many scholars emend even to avir (reading two occurrences of avir by haplography). The translation retains MT with a comma separating the two titles: "the Shepherd, the Stone of Israel." The LXX renders τὸν Ισραηλ, simply "Israel," by reading even differently, possibly as a verbal form (or collapsing the phrase). The title "the Stone of Israel" anticipates the cornerstone imagery of Ps 118:22 and later messianic interpretation.

**9. birkhot shadayim va-racham (49:25) — "blessings of the breasts and of the womb" [בִּרְכֹת שָׁדַיִם וָרָחַם]**
The blessing formula in v. 25 is structured in three couplets: heavens above / deep below // breasts / womb. The pairing shadayim va-racham (breasts and womb) is the only such explicit fertility blessing in the patriarchal narratives, and the wordplay with El Shaddai directly preceding is deliberate: Shaddai — shadayim ("breasts") share the root shad or a near-homophone. Whether El Shaddai etymologically means "God of the breast/mountain/plain" remains contested, but the author activates the sonic resonance. The Joseph oracle thus contains within one sentence both the name El Shaddai and the word shadayim, making the blessing of fertility a kind of signature of the name. The translation preserves the plain anatomical meaning.

**10. Gad gedud yegudenu (49:19) — Triple wordplay on Gad [גָּד גְּדוּד יְגוּדֶנּוּ]**
The oracle on Gad is a tour de force of Hebrew paronomasia: the tribal name Gad, the noun gedud ("raiding band"), and the verb yegudenu ("shall raid him") are all built on the same triliteral root gdl/gdd. The oracle reads in Hebrew almost like a riddle in sound: "Gad — a raiding-band shall raid him — but he shall raid at their heel." The word egev ("heel") in the final clause adds a further resonance with Jacob's own name (Ya'aqov from aqev, "heel"), connecting Gad's oracle to the founding patriarch. The LXX renders Γαδ πειρατήριον πειρατεύσει αὐτόν, "Gad — a raiding host shall raid him," capturing two of the three cognates.

**11. nazir echav (49:26) — "the consecrated one of his brothers" [נְזִיר אֶחָיו]**
The word nazir means "one who is set apart, consecrated, dedicated" — the root of the Nazirite vow (Num 6). Applied to Joseph, it means he who was separated from his brothers — both in the historical sense (sold to Egypt, removed from Canaan) and in the consecrated sense (set apart for a unique divine purpose). The word nazir carries both the connotation of separation and of consecration; the translation "the consecrated one of his brothers" foregrounds the sacred designation (nazir as one dedicated to a divine purpose), while the context of the Joseph narrative provides the note of separation. Alternative: "the prince among his brothers" (taking nazir from a related root meaning "to crown"). LXX renders the verse differently and translates nazir with ἡγιασμένος among brothers — "the holy one among his brothers" — emphasizing cultic consecration over the separation motif, aligning with the "consecrated one" rendering here.

**12. vayigva vaye'aseph el-ammav (49:33) — "he breathed his last and was gathered to his people" [וַיִּגְוַע וַיֵּאָסֶף אֶל-עַמָּיו]**
Two verbs describe Jacob's death: gava (to breathe out one's last breath, expire; cf. Gen 25:8, 17; 35:29) and ne'esaph el-ammav (to be gathered to one's people). The verb gava is more physical — the last breath; ne'esaph is communal and theological — reunion with the ancestral dead in Sheol or the realm of the dead. The phrase ne'esaph el-ammav echoes the deaths of Abraham (25:8), Ishmael (25:17), Isaac (35:29), and Aaron (Num 20:24), forming a refrain across the patriarchal narrative. The translation preserves both verbs without harmonizing them into a single "died." Jacob also "gathers his feet into the bed" (v. 33a) — a final act of self-composure before death, mirroring the gathering of his household at the chapter's opening (v. 1, he'asephu).

**13. mekhokek mi-bein raglav (49:10) — "the ruler's staff from between his feet" [וּמְחֹקֵק מִבֵּין רַגְלָיו]**
The noun mekhokek, from khaqaq ("to engrave, decree"), means "lawgiver, ruler, one who decrees." The phrase "from between his feet" is either a concrete image (a staff held between the feet or planted on the ground before a seated ruler) or a euphemistic idiom for progeny (raglayim, "feet," sometimes functioning as a body-part euphemism as in Ruth 3:4, Isa 7:20). Both readings are grammatically possible; both point to the same concept: the Judahite royal line will continue until Shiloh. The oracle thus doubly reinforces Judah's royal preeminence through two parallel instruments of authority: the shevet (scepter/rod) and the mekhokek (the ruler's staff/the lawgiver himself).

## LXX Comparison Table (MT vs. Codex Vaticanus)

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Note |
|-------|---------------------|-----------------|------|
| 49:1 | "in the latter days" [בְּאַחֲרִית הַיָּמִים] | "in the last of the days" (ἐπ' ἐσχάτων τῶν ἡμερῶν) | LXX uses stronger eschatological wording |
| 49:4 | "reckless as water" [פַּחַז כַּמַּיִם] | "you acted wantonly/insolently as water" (ἐξύβρισας ὡς ὕδωρ) | LXX interprets the hapax pachaz as hubris/wantonness, using a finite verb; MT is nominal |
| 49:5 | "instruments of violence" [כְּלֵי חָמָס] — "their swords" [מְכֵרֹתֵיהֶם] | "they completed their iniquity by choice" (συνετέλεσαν ἀδικίαν ἐξ αἱρέσεως αὐτῶν) | Major divergence; LXX reflects a different Vorlage for mekherteihem |
| 49:6 | "let not my glory be joined" [אַל-תֵּחַד כְּבֹדִי] | "let not my liver (=inmost being) join in" (μὴ ἔλθῃ ἡ ψυχή μου εἰς βουλὴν αὐτῶν) | LXX replaces kavod ("glory") with psyche ("soul"); likely variant or theological softening |
| 49:10 | "until Shiloh comes" [עַד כִּי-יָבֹא שִׁילֹה] | "until the things reserved for him come" (ἕως ἂν ἔλθῃ τὰ ἀποκείμενα αὐτῷ) | LXX interprets "Shiloh" as "what is laid up for him" — the "whose it is" reading |
| 49:10 | "the obedience of the peoples" [יִקְּהַת עַמִּים] | "the expectation of the nations" (προσδοκία ἐθνῶν) | LXX renders yiqhat as "expectation/hope" rather than "obedience" |
| 49:14 | "a strong donkey" [חֲמֹר גָּרֶם] | "a fine beast of burden" (ὑποζύγιον καλόν) | LXX loses the "bony/sturdy" nuance of garem |
| 49:17 | "a horned viper" [שְׁפִיפֹן] | "a basilisk" (κεράστης) | LXX shephiphon = horned snake; LXX kerastes = a specific horned viper; broadly equivalent |
| 49:18 | "For your salvation I wait, O LORD" [לִישׁוּעָתְךָ קִוִּיתִי יְהוָה] | "I wait for the salvation of the LORD" (τὴν σωτηρίαν περιμένω κυρίου) | MT: direct address (O LORD); LXX: third-person reference; both express the prayer-interjection |
| 49:22 | "a son of fruitfulness by a spring" [בֵּן פֹּרָת עֲלֵי-עָיִן] | "my son is increased" (υἱός μου ηὐξημένος) | LXX renders ben porat as "an increased/grown son"; MT ben porat is literally "son of fruitfulness," preserving the pun on Ephraim; LXX loses the vine/spring imagery |
| 49:24 | "the Mighty One of Jacob" [אֲבִיר יַעֲקֹב] | "by the hand of the Mighty One of Jacob" (ὑπὸ χεῖρα δυνάστου Ιακωβ) | LXX agrees on avir = dynast/mighty one; adds "by the hand of" |
| 49:24 | "the Shepherd, the Stone of Israel" [רֹעֶה אֶבֶן יִשְׂרָאֵל] | "from there is the one who strengthened Israel" (ἐκεῖθεν ὁ κατισχύσας Ισραηλ) | LXX conflates/rewrites; loses the Shepherd and Stone titles |
| 49:25 | "El Shaddai" [שַׁדַּי] | "the God of your father" (θεοῦ πατρός σου) | LXX drops the untranslated Shaddai; absorbs into the preceding divine title |
| 49:26 | "the everlasting hills" [גִּבְעֹת עוֹלָם] | "the everlasting hills" (ὀρέων ἀΐδιων) | Agreement; LXX aidion = everlasting |
| 49:26 | "the consecrated one of his brothers" [נְזִיר אֶחָיו] | "the holy one among his brothers" (ἡγιασμένου ἐν ἀδελφοῖς) | LXX nazir = hagiasmenou ("consecrated/holy"); both MT and LXX emphasize sacred consecration; the preposition differs (construct chain "of" vs. ἐν "among") |
| 49:27 | "a wolf that tears" [זְאֵב יִטְרָף] / "he divides plunder" [יְחַלֵּק שָׁלָל] | "a ravening wolf" (λύκος ἅρπαξ) / "he divides the spoil" (μεριεῖ σκῦλα) | MT ze'ev yitraf is literally "a wolf — it tears" (yitraf from taraf, to tear/rend); LXX harpax = "grasping/seizing" rather than "tearing," shifting from tearing violence to seizure |

---

## Structural Note

Genesis 49 is the poetic and theological climax of the entire book of Genesis. It is the longest sustained poem in the patriarchal narratives and arguably the most archaic Hebrew in the entire Pentateuch, preserving linguistic features and divine epithets that antedate most of the prose surrounding it.

**The Architectonics of the Twelve Oracles:**

The blessing is not arranged in birth order alone, nor by mother alone, but in a sequence with deliberate rhetorical structure. The chapter divides into three movements of unequal weight:

*Movement 1 — The Forfeited First-Born (vv. 3–7):* Three sons of Leah are treated negatively — Reuben (forfeits the birthright), Simeon and Levi (cursed for the Shechem massacre). This opening movement establishes that proximity to Jacob's blessing is not determined by birth order or lineage alone but by character. The curse of Simeon and Levi (to be scattered in Israel) is historically fulfilled: Simeon was absorbed into Judah's territory (Josh 19:1–9), and Levi received no tribal allotment but was distributed as the priestly class (Num 18). The blessing-as-curse form is a distinct subgenre.

*Movement 2 — The Extended Royal Oracle (vv. 8–12):* Judah receives by far the longest oracle of any of the twelve — five verses of dense, imagistic poetry containing the Shiloh clause, the lion imagery, and the vine-country abundance. The oracle's length signals Judah's preeminence, and the Shiloh clause (v. 10) transforms the tribal blessing into a dynastic and ultimately messianic oracle. The scepter-and-staff couplet, the lion imagery (anticipating the "Lion of Judah" of Rev 5:5), the Shiloh controversy, and the wine-and-milk abundance of the land establish Judah as the chapter's central theological weight. The five brief oracles that follow (Zebulun through Naphtali, vv. 13–21) serve as foils that throw Judah's length into relief.

*Movement 3 — The Joseph Oracle (vv. 22–26):* Joseph receives the second-longest oracle, in keeping with his narrative centrality across Genesis 37–50. The Joseph oracle is distinguished by its stacking of divine titles — four in sequence, a density unique in the Hebrew Bible: avir Ya'akov, ro'eh even Yisra'el, El Avikha, El Shaddai. The triple blessing formula (heavens above / deep below / breasts and womb) functions as a comprehensive cosmic blessing, surveying the totality of the created order as the source of Joseph's prosperity. The nazir epithet at the oracle's close ties Joseph's separation to a sacred vocation.

**The Benjamin Closure (v. 27):** Benjamin's brief, fierce oracle — "a wolf that tears, in the morning he devours prey" — closes the twelve with a startling martial image that anticipates Saul (a Benjaminite) and the later tribe's warrior reputation (Judg 20; 1 Sam 9). The brevity and violence contrast with the expansive blessing of Joseph, forming a final couplet of opposite temperaments: the favored son and the fierce youngest.

**The Double Gathering (vv. 1, 33):** The chapter opens with Jacob commanding his sons to "gather" (he'asephu, v. 1) and closes with Jacob himself being "gathered" to his people (ne'esaph el-ammav, v. 33). The Hebrew root asaph governs both movements: the patriarch gathers his family for the final time, then is himself gathered into the company of the dead. Between these two gatherings, the entire history of Israel's twelve tribes is prophetically sketched in miniature — the closest the book of Genesis comes to the theology of Deuteronomy or the sweep of the Deuteronomistic History.

**The Absence of Rachel:** The narrative poignancy of the burial command (vv. 29–31) lies in the list of those buried at Machpelah: Abraham and Sarah, Isaac and Rebekah, Leah — but not Rachel, buried on the road to Bethlehem (Gen 35:19–20). Jacob's request "bury me with my fathers... where I buried Leah" (vv. 29–31) acknowledges the structural asymmetry of his loves and his losses. Rachel, the beloved wife, is absent from the ancestral tomb; Leah, the unloved, is there to receive him. The text does not editorialize; it records.

**Theological Unity:** The chapter holds together the largest span of Israel's future — tribal territories, royal lineage, priestly dispersal, military prowess, agricultural abundance — within the frame of a dying father's final words. The form is testament (tzava'ah), the content is prophecy (nevi'ah), and the theology is covenant: what Jacob blesses, God will enact. The dying patriarch speaks with the authority of the divine promise given to Abraham, confirmed to Isaac, and wrestled to Jacob at the Jabbok — and now transmitted, in differentiated form, to all twelve sons who will become twelve peoples.
